concept_name: Initial Conditions — Fixing the Integration Constants
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 7.4 — Boundary Conditions; split from non_uniform_acceleration bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: Two cars leave opposite ends of the same road — one starts from Delhi at 10 AM moving south at 60 km/h, the other starts from Agra at the same time moving north at 70 km/h. Both cars obey the same simple equation: position equals starting point plus speed times time. But the solutions differ because the starting points differ. The kinematic equations describe the family of ALL possible motions. Initial conditions pick out the ONE motion that actually happened in your problem.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A ball tossed upward from the roof of a building. Another ball thrown upward from ground level with the same initial speed. Both balls obey a equals minus g. Both have the same equation of motion. But their positions at any later time differ — because one started six metres higher than the other. The integration constant for y captures the starting height.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Two cricket balls hit with the same swing speed at Eden Gardens, but one is already moving in the direction of the swing and the other is stationary when the bat meets it. After the hit, both accelerate identically, but their speeds and positions thereafter differ. The initial velocity is one of the two integration constants.

formulas.two_constants: A 2nd-order equation (like d^2x/dt^2 = a) produces TWO integration constants
formulas.typical_choice: The constants are traditionally x(0) and v(0) — called INITIAL CONDITIONS
formulas.kinematic_example: x(t) = x0 + u t + 0.5 a t^2   — x0 is position at t = 0, u is velocity at t = 0
formulas.variable_accel_example: For a(t) = 2t:  v(t) = u + t^2,  x(t) = x0 + u t + t^3/3   — x0 and u are the integration constants

constraints: Every integration introduces a new unknown constant of integration
Two integrations (from a to v, and v to x) need TWO initial conditions
Without initial conditions, the solution is a FAMILY of motions, not a specific one
Initial conditions are supplied by the problem (e.g. 'starts at rest', 'thrown from height 5 m')

STATE_1.title: Why Integration Needs a Starting Point

scene_composition.text:
Same road, same SPEEDS, different STARTING POINTS

[DELHI]                         [AGRA]
  CAR 1                           CAR 2
  -> 60 km/h (southward)          <- 70 km/h (northward)
  starts at x = 0                 starts at x = 200 km

Both cars obey the SAME equation:
  position = starting_position + speed * time

But their SOLUTIONS differ:
  x_1(t) = 0 + 60 t
  x_2(t) = 200 - 70 t

Same physics, different initial positions.
The starting point is the INITIAL CONDITION.

tts_sentences.text_en: Two cars set off from opposite ends of a road. Car one from Delhi, car two from Agra. Both drive at steady speeds.
They obey the same type of equation — position equals starting point plus speed times time. Same formula, same physics.
Yet at any later time, they are at very different positions. Car one's x grows from zero. Car two's x shrinks from two hundred toward Delhi.

What separates the two solutions is not the equation but the starting point. That is what an initial condition does — it picks out one specific motion from the family of all motions that obey the same equation.
Every time you integrate, you introduce a new unknown constant. To solve for it, you need a data point — usually given by the problem as 'starts at rest' or 'released from height h'.

STATE_2.title: Two Integrations, Two Constants — How They Get Fixed

scene_composition.text:
Motion equation: a(t) = 2 t

First integration (a -> v)
  dv/dt = 2 t
  dv = 2 t dt
  v(t) = t^2 + C_1      <- FIRST integration constant

  To fix C_1, we need 'v at some specific t'.
  The problem says: at t = 0, v = u.
  Plug in.  u = 0 + C_1 = C_1.
  So  C_1 = u.

  v(t) = u + t^2

Second integration (v -> x)
  dx/dt = u + t^2
  dx = (u + t^2) dt
  x(t) = u t + t^3 / 3 + C_2    <- SECOND integration constant

  Problem says: at t = 0, x = x0.
  Plug in.  x0 = 0 + 0 + C_2 = C_2.
  So  C_2 = x0.

  x(t) = x0 + u t + t^3 / 3
Two Integrations, Two Constants — How They Get Fixed
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Take the example a of t equals two t. Two successive integrations give us v and x, but each introduces an unknown constant.
First integration. v equals t squared plus C-one, where C-one is our first unknown constant.

Now we need one piece of data to find C-one. The problem gives it — at t equals zero, velocity is u. Plug in. u equals zero plus C-one, so C-one equals u. Our velocity formula is v equals u plus t squared.
Second integration. x equals u t plus t cubed over three plus C-two — a second unknown constant.
Again we need one data point. At t equals zero, position is x-zero. Plug in. x-zero equals C-two. So C-two equals x-zero.
Final formula — x of t equals x-zero plus u t plus t cubed over three. Every piece is determined. Two integration constants, two initial conditions, problem solved.

STATE_3.title: How Many Initial Conditions Do You Need?

scene_composition.text:
Counting rule

Every integration introduces one unknown constant.

For kinematics:
  - a(t) -> v(t)   needs 1 initial condition (v0 or u)
  - v(t) -> x(t)   needs 1 initial condition (x0)
  Total   =   2

For a 2nd-order ODE like m d^2x/dt^2 = F(x, v, t)
  Two integrations  ->  two constants  ->  two initial conditions.
  Usually given as (x0, v0).

For 3rd-order systems (rare)
  three constants  ->  three initial conditions.

Mnemonic
  Number of constants = order of the differential equation
                     = number of integrations.
How Many Initial Conditions Do You Need?
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: The counting rule — every integration introduces one unknown constant, and each constant needs one piece of initial data to determine.
For kinematics — one integration from a to v, one from v to x. Two integrations, two constants, two initial conditions.

The two conditions are usually given as initial velocity and initial position at t equals zero. Problem phrases such as 'released from rest' tell you v equals zero; 'from a height of five metres' tells you x equals five.

A second-order differential equation — which is what Newton's law is for particle motion — always needs exactly two initial conditions. Higher-order equations need more, but these are rare in mechanics.
The mnemonic — number of constants equals order of the differential equation equals number of integrations. In standard kinematics, that number is always two.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Skip the +C after integrating — the result is still correct
trigger_phrases: does it matter if I skip +C

STATE_1.title: Your Shortcut — Integrate and Skip the +C

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

In kinematics, I see
  dv = 2t dt
  v = t^2

I did not write + C.
Does it matter?

Example
  If block STARTS at v = 5 m/s,
  and I write v = t^2,
  then at t = 0 I get v = 0, NOT 5.
  
  My answer is wrong by 5 m/s FOREVER.
Your Shortcut — Integrate and Skip the +C
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: A common shortcut — skip the integration constant after integrating. 'Plus C' feels like algebra clutter.
The problem is that the constant carries real physical information. Without it, your answer is exactly wrong by the starting value.
If the block actually started at five metres per second, writing v equals t squared would give zero at t equals zero — five units too low, forever.

STATE_2.title: Why +C Matters — The Universe of Wrong Answers

scene_composition.text:
Without +C
  v = t^2
  (one specific curve starting at 0)

With +C
  v = t^2 + C
  (a whole FAMILY of curves, one per C)

If the problem says 'starts at rest'
  v(0) = 0 = 0^2 + C  ->  C = 0
  Answer: v = t^2.

If the problem says 'starts at 5 m/s'
  v(0) = 5 = 0^2 + C  ->  C = 5
  Answer: v = t^2 + 5.

If the problem says 'starts at -3 m/s'
  C = -3, so v = t^2 - 3.

The +C is the HANDLE that lets the math
tune itself to any specific scenario.
Dropping it silently assumes the object
starts at rest -- a huge implicit assumption.
Why +C Matters — The Universe of Wrong Answers
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Without the plus C, integration gives one specific curve. With it, you have a whole family of curves — one per value of C.

The initial condition picks which member of the family applies to this problem. Starts at rest — C equals zero. Starts at five metres per second — C equals five. Starts at minus three — C equals minus three.
Skipping the plus C silently fixes C equal to zero, which is equivalent to assuming the object starts at rest. For most problems, that is an unwarranted implicit assumption.
Write the plus C every time. Then use the initial condition to solve for it. That discipline costs one extra line and saves whole classes of wrong answers.

STATE_3.title: The Habit — Write C, Then Kill It With Data

scene_composition.text:
Recipe

(1) Integrate. Write +C_1.
(2) Plug in the first initial condition.
    Solve for C_1. Replace.
(3) Integrate again. Write +C_2.
(4) Plug in the second initial condition.
    Solve for C_2. Replace.
(5) Final formula has no unknown constants.

Every kinematics problem with variable a
follows this 5-step structure. Make it a reflex.
The Habit — Write C, Then Kill It With Data
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Make this a reflex. Integrate, write plus C, plug in initial condition, kill the C. Integrate again, write plus C-two, plug in, kill it.
After two cycles, your final formula has no unknown constants. It is a specific x-of-t that matches the problem exactly.
Every non-uniform-acceleration problem follows this five-step template. Master the template once, and the individual problem details become routine.

regeneration_variants.label: Different starting conditions
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Same equation a = 2t, but block starts at x0 = 10 m with u = 5 m/s. v(t) = 5 + t^2, x(t) = 10 + 5t + t^3/3. Shifted curves compared to x0=0, u=0 case.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same equation, different initial conditions -> different specific solutions

regeneration_variants.label: Conditions given at non-zero t
regeneration_variants.physical_world: If problem gives v(2) = 10 instead of v(0), back-substitute to find C. v(t) = t^2 + C; at t=2, 4 + C = 10 -> C = 6. So v(t) = t^2 + 6, and v(0) = 6.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Initial conditions can be specified at any time, not only t = 0
